_meta.purpose: Vendored, normalized subset of public anti-analysis primitive APIs + byte sequences used by the re-anti-analysis MCP server. Vendor-neutral: categories only, no product or vendor names.
_meta.arch_default_note: A15 fix (v2.8.0): every entry is implicitly scoped to x86/x86_64 unless an explicit `archs` field overrides it. The disasm pass in re-anti-analysis must skip x86-only entries when the target binary is AArch64/ARM32/etc. (the r03-stress APK pass produced 306 false-positive INT-3 hits on libproot.so/libzstd-jni because the x86 byte-pattern catalog was applied to AArch64 binaries).

category_legend.anti_debug: Primitives the binary uses to detect or interfere with debuggers (user-mode only).
category_legend.anti_attach: Primitives that prevent or detect a debugger attaching to the running process.
category_legend.anti_vm: Primitives the binary uses to detect a virtual machine (CPUID leaves, hypervisor bits, ACPI/SMBIOS probes).
category_legend.anti_emulator: Primitives that detect a CPU emulator (QEMU, Unicorn, Speakeasy) by timing skew, missing CPUID leaves, or behavior.
category_legend.anti_sandbox: Primitives that detect a sandbox (CAPE, Triage, Joe Sandbox) by file/process/registry/user-interaction probes.
category_legend.process_introspection: Primitives that read the host's process list, handle table, or module list.
category_legend.memory_integrity: Primitives that read or verify the integrity of the process's own memory (text-section hashing, self-checksumming).
category_legend.code_integrity: Primitives that install hook detection, IAT patching detection, or inline-hook detection.

evidence_legend.api_name: API name appears in the binary's string table (string_only confirmation per anti_debug_indicators).
evidence_legend.import: API is in the IAT (import_only confirmation).
evidence_legend.byte_sequence: A canonical byte sequence (e.g. 0F 31 for RDTSC). Requires disasm to count.
evidence_legend.symbol: A debug symbol or string literal (e.g. /prodid, MachineGuid).

entries.note: PEB.BeingDebugged reader; the canonical user-mode anti-debug primitive. Single-primitive match in isolation is high-FP (debug-build heuristics); requires co-occurrence with another ad-* primitive to be a real signal.
Cross-process debugger detection via kernel32.
Used with ProcessDebugPort / ProcessDebugObjectHandle / ProcessDebugFlags to detect debug attach.
Often used as a debugger-attach tripwire (debugger receives the string; non-debugger context silently swallows it).
Read time-stamp counter. High-FP in isolation (legit timing code); counted as anti-debug only when paired with a spin loop and a debugger-presence check.
Debug exception that fires only under a debugger (the kernel delivers DBG_PRINTEXCEPTION_C). The native code path is a no-op; the debugger path increments RIP past the next instruction.
Software breakpoint opcode. Patched into target by debuggers; presence in a code path that is *not* a known debug stub is a strong anti-debug signal.
Direct read of the PEB.BeingDebugged byte at fs:[0x30] + 2. Common in encrypted-VM dispatchers that want to avoid importing IsDebuggerPresent.
Direct read of the PEB.NtGlobalFlag dword (fs:[0x30] + 0x68). Set by the kernel to 0x70 when the process is created under a debugger.
Anti-anti-debug side: enumerate debuggers attached by walking the process-debug-object handle table.
Create the debug object that NtDebugActiveProcess requires. If the call returns a non-NULL handle, the process is being debugged.
Resume from a debug exception without yielding control to the debugger. High-FP in isolation.
CPUID leaf 1 ECX bit 31 = 1 when running under a hypervisor. Cross-platform standard VM-detection primitive.

CPUID leaf 0x40000000 returns the hypervisor vendor signature (e.g. 'Microsoft Hv', 'VMwareVMware', 'KVMKVMKVM', 'TCGTCGTCGTCG'). Note: signature strings are excluded from this catalog; only the opcode is recorded.
TSC skew between two back-to-back RDTSCs is a strong VM-detection signal (emulators and some hypervisors introduce 10-100x slowdown on the second read).
VMXON and VMCALL are ring 0 in real hardware. Their presence in userland code is a hypervisor-detection or hypervisor-deployment primitive (anti-RE/anti-cheat use case).
Reads SMBIOS tables and looks for vendor strings (e.g. 'VMware', 'QEMU', 'Microsoft Corporation'). Note: signature strings are excluded from this catalog; only the SYMBOL token is recorded.
Reads ACPI tables (\\.\\ACPI#...) to detect virtual hardware. Often paired with SMBIOS probe (av-005).
Open \\.\\PhysicalDrive0 to read disk serial. The disk-serial string is one of the canonical VM-fingerprinting primitives (the VM's serial is often a known constant).

Reads HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\VMware to detect VMware Tools / VMware hypervisor. Note: this is the Windows-side catalog of a single hypervisor; the analyst's job is to map the category to the *current* target.

Reads the NIC's MAC address and checks against the IEEE OUI database for known VM-assigned prefixes (00:0C:29 = VMware, 00:1C:14, 08:00:27 = QEMU/KVM). Note: OUI prefixes are excluded from this catalog; only the SYMBOL token is recorded.
Sleep(INFINITE) hangs the emulator (no timeout). Anti-emulator primitive; in a real process the call returns when the system is shut down.
Reads CPUID leaf 0x40000100; QEMU and Unicorn return 0 in EAX/EBX/ECX/EDX, real hardware returns the leaf-specific value. Cheap anti-emulator primitive.
Iterate the IAT of ntdll.dll and look for hooks. Emulators often present a stub IAT; real Windows has the full set.
Sleep + read cursor position; if unchanged after a long sleep, the process is in a sandbox (no human interaction).
Looks for the Cuckoo / CAPE pipe path. Note: the Cuckoo path string is excluded from this catalog; only the SYMBOL token is recorded.
Reads the username + computername via GetUserNameW + GetComputerNameW and checks against a short whitelist of known sandbox names.
Calls NtDelayExecution / usleep and checks that the elapsed wall-clock time matches the requested sleep. Sandboxes often fast-forward sleeps.
Enumerate the process list. The anti-analysis use is to look for analysis-tool process names.
Native API equivalent of CreateToolhelp32Snapshot. The encrypted-VM dispatcher uses this to walk the process list looking for debuggers and analysis tools.
Enumerate loaded modules in a target process. Anti-analysis use is to look for analysis-tool DLL names.
Walk a process's address space. Encrypted-VM packers use this to find the unpacked code region after the lazy-decrypt stub runs.
Hashes the .text section at runtime and compares to a known-good value. Encrypted-VM packers use this to detect in-memory patching.
Walk the IAT at runtime; if a function pointer doesn't match the file offset, the IAT has been hooked.
Read the first byte of every function in the IAT; if it's 0xE9 (JMP rel32) or 0x68 0x?? 0x?? 0x?? 0x?? (PUSH imm32), the function has been hooked.
